questions.text: I find that working behind the scenes for others' benefit is more satisfying than being in the spotlight.
I tend to choose mentors and advisors based on how they can accelerate my personal growth.
I prefer fluid, evolving roles in collaborative work where people contribute where they can.
I find that I read social situations the way a musician reads a score, perceiving multiple layers at once.
I notice that even subtle shifts in atmosphere — a room going quiet, a change in tone — hit me with force.
I feel that suffering's moral priority is the one ethical principle I have never seriously doubted.
I find that my emotions arrive at a volume that allows me to observe them clearly.
I find that setting deadlines for myself, even arbitrary ones, keeps me productive.
I notice that my emotional reactions to the same type of event are remarkably consistent over time.
I tend to let conversations flow where the group takes them.
I am drawn to founding organizations rather than joining existing ones.
I tend to scope out my territory in any collaborative effort.
I believe that emotional composure during another's crisis is a form of care that prioritizes effective action over visible sympathy.
I find that I am usually the last person to notice when the mood in a room has shifted.
I naturally distribute decision-making power more broadly as projects progress.
I feel a responsibility to contribute more than my fair share.
I find that I do not naturally read subtext in emails or text messages.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the reality that gives moral philosophy its purpose and its urgency.
I notice when the rhythm of someone's texting changes, even slightly, and read meaning into it.
I regularly redirect my personal budget toward helping people around me.
I can be in the midst of uncertainty and still feel fundamentally okay.
I experience emotional pain as something sharp and physical, not abstract.
I deal with passwords as they come up and reset them when I forget.
I hold the view that my ambitions are a source of vitality and meaning.
I notice that when multiple emotions arrive at once, the overload can feel physically paralyzing.
I find that when I engage with difficult situations analytically, I can see possibilities for improvement that are invisible to those caught up in the emotion of the moment.
I usually adjust my stance to keep the group aligned.
I think that composure during emotional events is a prerequisite for the kind of thinking that leads to real solutions.
I pick up on the difference between someone who pauses to think and someone who pauses for dramatic effect.
I feel that planning for worst-case scenarios is practical, not pessimistic.
I keep my digital files organized in a clear folder hierarchy.
I feel that I have a right to put myself first without apology.
I frequently take on captain or leadership roles in team settings.
I feel comfortable dropping a project when my interest shifts to something more compelling.
When I hear about systemic injustice, I feel a moral imperative to contribute to change, not just to be informed.
I find it puzzling when people say one thing but apparently mean another.
